title: How This Mouse Lives at a Higher Elevation Than Any Other Vertebrate
gist: A Science study explains how the Andean leaf-eared mouse survives at 22,000 feet, the highest of any vertebrate, via boosted maximal oxygen consumption, ramped thermogenesis, and heart-protective genes seen in highland vs lowland genomes.

insights.claim: The Andean leaf-eared mouse (Phyllotis vaccarum) lives as high as 22,000 feet, on the summit of Volcán Llullaillaco at 22,110 feet, where oxygen is 44 percent of sea level, the highest elevation of any vertebrate.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A record-setting case study in physiological limits.

insights.claim: A Science study compared genomes of 167 leaf-eared mice from lowlands (~4,600 ft) and highlands (~14,000 ft); highland mice showed greater maximal oxygen consumption than lowland relatives.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Direct genomic evidence of altitude adaptation.
insights.locator: In the new study, researchers compared

insights.claim: Highland mice ramped up thermogenesis, with more active shivering enzymes and more metabolically active heat-generating fat, plus gene activity protecting the heart from hypertension while pumping harder.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Details the multi-system adaptation toolkit for cold and hypoxia.

insights.claim: The mice evolved extra-large ears to communicate in ultra-high-frequency squeaks that carry in the lower-density air of high altitude.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Shows sensory adaptation alongside metabolic adaptation.
